reason: The abstract's headline conclusion rests directly on RF importance magnitude/ranking ("SSH and SST...were the most important predictors"), and Section 3.5/4.4 build a full most/moderately/least-important tier list for each species purely from the scaled importance percentages in Figure 6b. The Methods (2.6) never specify importance.mode; they cite the classic 'randomForest' package (whose default importance() is Gini/MeanDecreaseGini) fit "through ranger for speed," and pair it only with Altmann et al.'s permutation-of-response significance test — which is the standard corrective device applied to impurity/Gini importance in the ecology literature and, per the task's own framing, only certifies importance != 0, not rank reliability. Figure 6a's "influence" curves are PDP-like shape plots shown alongside importance, but the text never uses their amplitude to cross-validate the Gini-based ranking, no permutation (Breiman-type) importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature subset is reported, and known bias drivers (chlorophyll-a's skew, hour's 24 categorical levels, evidenced by the GVIF/aGSIF table) are present. So the ranking is central and uncorroborated, and impurity-style importance is the best-supported reading of what was actually computed, though the paper's silence on importance.mode leaves some residual uncertainty on that specific point.
